subclass.name: Spirits Domain
subclassFeatures: Channel Divinity: Back From the Brink|Cleric|PHB|Spirits|SMRClerics|2

subclassFeature.name: Artifice Domain
entries: The Artifice Domain focuses on holy crafts, channeling the will of the gods into miraculous creations. These priests look down on {@subclass Forge|Cleric|PHB|XGE|clerics of the forge} as merely jumped-up blacksmiths, while they dismiss {@subclass knowledge|Cleric|PHB|PHB|knowledge priests} as insufficiently practical. An Artifice priest might spend their days preaching, and then stay up the night tinkering. While often clever, their ingenuity is so striking as to seem divinely inspired; a mortal mind would not make such leaps of logic.

Priests of the Artifice Domain may be of any alignment, most often inspired by creation for creation's sake. In the Forgotten Realms, they will most often be found worshiping {@deity Gond|Faerûnian|SCAG}, though they are also common among {@deity Moradin|Dwarven|MTF|Moradin's} clergy or any of the gnomish pantheon.

entries.name: Artifice Domain Quirks
entries: The following are some optional quirks for a player of this domain to choose from.

rows: You speak to your tools.
You prefer to clean your crafting tools with {@item holy water (flask)|PHB|holy water}.
You say a prayer for every crafter you meet.
You add pointless gears and knobs to your equipment.
You say a penance anytime one of your constructions fails.
Your {@item holy symbol|PHB} has intricate hinges and can be folded into different shapes based on your mood.

subclassFeature.name: Domain Spells
entries: You gain domain spells at the cleric levels listed in the Artifice Domain Spells table. See the {@classFeature Divine Domain|Cleric|PHB|1} class feature for how domain spells work.

entries.caption: Artifice Domain Spells
rows: 1st
{@spell color spray}, {@spell unseen servant}
3rd
{@spell dancing objects (animate object)|KibblesTasty:CC|dancing object}, {@spell shatter}
5th
{@spell protection from energy}, {@spell tiny servant|XGE}
7th
{@spell repair|KibblesTasty:CC}, {@spell summon construct|TCE}
9th
{@spell creation}, {@spell skill empowerment|XGE}

subclassFeature.name: Holy Crafts
entries: At 1st level, you learn the {@spell mending} cantrip, which counts as a cleric spell for you. You also gain proficiency with {@item thieves' tools|PHB} and two sets of {@item artisan's tools|PHB} of your choice. Anytime you make a check involving tools with which you are proficient, you may choose to use your Wisdom modifier in place of the ability you would normally use.

entries.name: Note: Crafting & Artifice
entries: The Artifice Domain works particularly well in games that incorporate a robust crafting ruleset; {@loader KibblesTasty's|collection/KibblesTasty; Kibbles' Crafting Guide} is the author's preferred choice{@homebrew |, linked at the bottom of this document}.

subclassFeature.name: Miraculous Artifice
entries: At 1st level, you gain the ability to imbue any weapon with a holy blessing as a bonus action. This blessing lasts for 1 hour, and turns the weapon's damage type into your choice of acid, cold, fire, or lightning. You can use this feature a number of times equal to your proficiency bonus, and you regain all expended uses when you finish a long rest. Only one weapon may be affected by your Miraculous Artifice at a time.

At 6th level, any creature dealing damage with an imbued weapon may choose to reroll the weapon's damage dice and choose the higher result. Additionally, you may now choose radiant or necrotic damage when you apply the blessing.

subclassFeature.name: Channel Divinity: Divine Inspiration
entries: Starting at 2nd level, as an action, you can use your {@classFeature Channel Divinity|Cleric|PHB|2} to touch a creature and grant them divine skill. For one hour, they have advantage on all crafting attempts and any ability checks involving tools.

subclassFeature.name: Sacred Contraption
entries: At 6th level, you are divinely inspired with an idea for a sacred contraption. You may spend ten minutes after completing a long rest to create a device capable of storing one of your {@subclassFeature Domain Spells|Cleric|PHB|Artifice|SMRClerics|1|SMRClerics|domain spells}. You choose the form and appearance of this contraption, though it always requires one hand for a creature to activate it. Any creature may cast the spell one time using the contraption, which uses your spellcasting ability and spellcasting modifier. The spell is always imbued at the highest spell level you can cast, up to 5th level. If the spell requires {@condition concentration}, then the creature who uses the contraption is the one who must maintain {@condition concentration}.
You may only have one Sacred Contraption at a time, and its magic expires after you complete a long rest.

subclassFeature.name: Divine Strike
entries: At 8th level, you gain the ability to infuse your weapon strikes with divine energy. Once on each of your turns when you hit a creature with a weapon attack, you can cause the attack to deal an extra {@damage 1d8} damage of the same type you last chose for your {@subclassFeature Miraculous Artifice|Cleric|PHB|Artifice|SMRClerics|1} feature. When you reach 14th level, the extra damage increases to {@damage 1d8}.

subclassFeature.name: Blessed Strikes
entries: {@i 8th-level cleric {@variantrule optional class features|TCE|optional feature}, which replaces the {@subclassFeature Divine Strike|Cleric|PHB|Artifice|SMRClerics|8} feature}

You are blessed with divine might in battle. When a creature takes damage from one of your cantrips or weapon attacks, you can also deal {@damage 1d8} radiant damage to that creature. Once you deal this damage, you can't use this feature again until the start of your next turn.

subclassFeature.name: Blessed Works
entries: At 17th level, creating or adjusting a {@subclassFeature Sacred Contraption|Cleric|PHB|Artifice|SMRClerics|6} now only takes you one minute. You may now create a number of {@subclassFeature Sacred Contraption|Cleric|PHB|Artifice|SMRClerics|6||Sacred Contraptions} up to your Wisdom modifier per long rest, though you can only have a given spell imbued once at a time. Once per long rest, you may remotely activate any of your {@subclassFeature Sacred Contraption|Cleric|PHB|Artifice|SMRClerics|6||Sacred Contraptions} within 60 feet as a bonus action.

subclassFeature.name: Empire Domain
entries: Some priests follow mortal authority as much as divine. The Empire domain is for clerics who exalt the throne, who seek to use their power to bolster a mighty ruler. Empire clerics revere the established order, and often view social change on par with blasphemy.

Priests of Empire are invariably rigid in their thinking, often more lawful than even the rulers they serve. Whether their path turns to good or evil depends substantially on the power in question, though strict adherence to the law can make monsters even out of the righteous.

entries.name: Empire Domain Quirks
entries: The following are some optional quirks for a player of this domain to choose from.

rows: Almost all your clothing is in imperial colors.
You always stand ram-rod straight, regardless of the circumstance.
You always keep a seasoning packet handy from your homeland.
You feel restless when not expanding your liege's domain.
You embroider an imperial insignia on all your garments. Even pajamas.
You sometimes have trouble telling fear and adoration apart.

subclassFeature.name: Domain Spells
entries: You gain domain spells at the cleric levels listed in the Empire Domain Spells table. See the {@classFeature Divine Domain|Cleric|PHB|1} class feature for how domain spells work.

entries.caption: Empire Domain Spells
rows: 1st
{@spell charm person}, {@spell command}
3rd
{@spell calm emotions}, {@spell suggestion}
5th
{@spell sending}, {@spell tongues}
7th
{@spell commandment|KibblesTasty:CC}, {@spell compulsion}
9th
{@spell dominate person}, {@spell wall of stone}

subclassFeature.name: Emissary of the Crown
entries: At 1st level, you gain the skills necessary to support your crown and its expansion. You gain proficiency in one skill out of {@skill Deception}, {@skill Intimidation}, and {@skill Persuasion}. When you use one of those skills in direct service to your liege, you can use your Wisdom modifier in place of Charisma.
Additionally, you gain proficiency with heavy armor.
"Direct service to your liege" would generally mean on direct orders from a figure in government, or as part of an official mission or state-sponsored action.

subclassFeature.name: Imperial Influence
entries: Additionally at 1st level, your commands carry the strength of a mighty power. When a creature succeeds on a saving throw against one of your enchantment spells of 1st level or higher, you can choose to make it suffer {@damage 1d6} psychic damage. A creature with complete immunity to a spell's effects does not suffer this damage.
This damage increases as your cleric level increases, to {@damage 2d6} at 5th level, {@damage 3d6} at 11th level, and {@damage 4d6} at 17th level.

subclassFeature.name: Channel Divinity: Voice of Empire
entries: Starting at 2nd level, you can use your {@classFeature Channel Divinity|Cleric|PHB|2} to extend your crown's influence. When you cast an enchantment spell with a casting time of 1 action, you can expend one use of your {@classFeature Channel Divinity|Cleric|PHB|2} to add one additional target.

subclassFeature.name: Soldier of the State
entries: Starting at 6th level, you gain the might to stand on the front line for your kingdom. When you take the {@action Attack} action, you can cast a cleric spell with a casting time of 1 action as a bonus action instead.

You can cast a spell in this way a number of times equal to your proficiency bonus, recovering all uses after finishing a long rest. If you want to cast a spell in this way when you have no uses remaining, you can do so by expending a spell slot one level higher than the spell's level, but the spell takes effect at its normal level. If you cast a cantrip when you have no uses remaining, it is cast as if your character level were 5 levels lower.

For example, to cast {@spell guiding bolt} (1st level) when you're out of uses, you would need to expend a 2nd-level slot. For a cantrip, a 6th-level cleric could cast {@spell sacred flame} as if they were 1st level. Effectively, you can think of this as "downcasting" the spell.

subclassFeature.name: Divine Strike
entries: At 8th level, you gain the ability to infuse your weapon strikes with divine energy. Once on each of your turns when you hit a creature with a weapon attack, you can cause the attack to deal an extra {@damage 1d8} damage of the same type dealt by the weapon to the target. When you reach 14th level, the extra damage increases to {@damage 2d8}.

subclassFeature.name: Blessed Strikes
entries: {@i 8th-level cleric {@variantrule optional class features|TCE|optional feature}, which replaces the {@subclassFeature Divine Strike|Cleric|PHB|Empire|SMRClerics|8} feature}

You are blessed with divine might in battle. When a creature takes damage from one of your cantrips or weapon attacks, you can also deal {@damage 1d8} radiant damage to that creature. Once you deal this damage, you can't use this feature again until the start of your next turn.

subclassFeature.name: Imperial Might
entries: At 17th level, creatures around you are tangibly affected by the sense of power you exude. While you are {@condition concentration||concentrating} on a spell, your weapon attacks are made with advantage.

subclassFeature.name: Empire Unconquered
entries: Also at 17th level, your magic allows no challenges to your authority. As a reaction when you would lose {@condition concentration} on a spell, you can choose a willing creature within 30 feet and inflict {@damage 2d6} psychic damage to it. You can add the damage dealt to the result of your saving throw to maintain {@condition concentration}, potentially changing the result.
Creatures that are {@condition charmed} or {@condition frightened} by you are considered willing for this effect.

subclassFeature.name: Plague Domain
entries: Priests of the Plague Domain are rare, and unwelcome. They are feared and reviled as plaguebringers, twisted clerics who exist to spread disease and rot. This reputation is often deserved, but not always. Some plague priests indeed seek to cause misery and suffering, but others aim to channel contagion where it may cause the least harm, or lead rites to honor gods of disease in hopes of staving off their wrath.

Priests of the Plague Domain are most often evil, to be sure, though exceptions exist. Even the purest-hearted are likely to be pragmatists who accept awful compromises. They are often solo wanderers, though they may find utility in joining with others on occasion.

entries.name: Plague Domain Quirks
entries: The following are some optional quirks for a player of this domain to choose from.

rows: You hiss and slobber when you speak.
Your skin is covered in rashes and boils, with inexplicable lumps that change from day to day.
You have a persistent, rattling cough.
You insist on saying a prayer before every meal. You friends wish you wouldn't.
You prioritize your appearance - it's important that others think you're as healthy as can be.
Your {@item holy symbol|PHB} rots away every few days and needs to be replaced.

subclassFeature.name: Domain Spells
entries: You gain domain spells at the cleric levels listed in the Plague Domain Spells table. See the {@classFeature Divine Domain|Cleric|PHB|1} class feature for how domain spells work.

entries.caption: Plague Domain Spells
rows: 1st
{@spell bane}, {@spell ray of sickness}
3rd
{@spell ray of enfeeblement}, {@spell touch of filth|SpellsThatDontSuck}
5th
{@spell bestow curse}, {@spell stinking cloud}
7th
{@spell poison puff|KibblesTasty:CC}, {@spell sickening radiance|XGE}
9th
{@spell inflict disease|SpellsThatDontSuck}, {@spell cloudkill}

subclassFeature.name: Miraculous Healer
entries: At 1st level, you gain proficiency with the {@skill Medicine} skill, and advantage on all Wisdom ({@skill Medicine}) checks to diagnose diseases.

In addition, you suffer no ill effects from nonmagical diseases (though you may still contract them). You can spend 1 minute praying over a creature suffering a nonmagical disease to transfer the disease to an uninfected creature you can see.

subclassFeature.name: Awful Transmutation
entries: Additionally at 1st level, as a bonus action whenever you cast a spell that deals poison damage, you may change its damage type to necrotic.

subclassFeature.name: Channel Divinity: Vile Contagion
entries: Starting at 2nd level, you can use your {@classFeature Channel Divinity|Cleric|PHB|2} to spread misery and suffering. As a reaction when a creature you can see within 60 feet is afflicted by a disease or the {@condition poisoned} condition, you can force another creature you can see within 60 feet to make a Wisdom saving throw. On a failure, that creature suffers the same affliction.

subclassFeature.name: Plaguebearer
entries: Starting at 6th level, you are a vessel for divine gifts. You gain resistance to poison damage. Additionally, when an enemy hits you with a melee attack, you can use your reaction to attempt to inflict a disease upon them. The target must make a Constitution saving throw against your spell save DC, or the attack's damage is halved and they are afflicted by one of the following diseases of your choice. Additionally, you can use your {@subclassFeature Channel Divinity: Vile Contagion|Cleric|PHB|Plague|SMRClerics|2||Vile Contagion} as part of this reaction.

You can afflict an enemy in this way a number of times equal to your Wisdom modifier, regaining all uses after a long rest. When a disease calls for a saving throw, it is against your spell save DC at the time the disease was inflicted.

entries.name: Bonewrack Fever
items: The afflicted creature's bones warp and splinter, causing agonizing pain. Their movement speed is immediately reduced by half, and when they take the {@action Dash} action, their maximum hit points are reduced by your cleric level. Every time they complete a long rest, they must make a Constitution saving throw. On a failure, their movement speed is reduced by 5 feet; when their speed reaches 0 due to this effect, they die. On a success, the disease ends.

entries.name: Muddlemind
items: The afflicted creature's thoughts get hazy and confused. At the start of each of their turns, they must roll a d6. On a roll of 1, they forget what they were doing and are {@condition incapacitated} until the end of their turn. On a roll of 2-3, they wander aimlessly, moving 10 feet in a random direction before taking their turn as normal. On a roll of 4-6, they can take their turn normally.

The creature can make a Wisdom saving throw at the end of each of their turns to suppress the disease's effects for 1 minute, but it returns afterwards. Every time they complete a long rest, they must make a Constitution saving throw. On a failure, their d6 rolls for Muddlemind suffer a cumulative -1 penalty. On a success, the disease ends.

entries.name: Crowpox
items: The afflicted creature begins to rot from the inside. They suffer disadvantage on all ability checks. Every time they complete a long rest, they must make a Constitution saving throw. On a failure, they suffer a cumulative -1 penalty to all ability checks. On a success, the disease ends.

subclassFeature.name: Divine Strike
entries: At 8th level, you gain the ability to infuse your weapon strikes with foul rot. Once on each of your turns when you hit a creature with a weapon attack, you can cause the attack to deal an extra {@damage 1d8} poison or necrotic damage. When you reach 14th level, the extra damage increases to {@damage 2d8}.

subclassFeature.name: Blessed Strikes
entries: {@i 8th-level cleric {@variantrule optional class features|TCE|optional feature}, which replaces the {@subclassFeature Divine Strike|Cleric|PHB|Plague|SMRClerics|8} feature}

You are blessed with divine might in battle. When a creature takes damage from one of your cantrips or weapon attacks, you can also deal {@damage 1d8} radiant damage to that creature. Once you deal this damage, you can't use this feature again until the start of your next turn.

subclassFeature.name: Improved Awful Transmutation
entries: Additionally at 8th level, your {@subclassFeature Awful Transmutation|Cleric|PHB|Plague|SMRClerics|1} ability no longer requires a bonus action to use.

subclassFeature.name: Malignant Aura
entries: At 17th level, your mere presence befouls the air. As a bonus action, you can force all creatures within 15 feet to make a Constitution saving throw or be {@condition poisoned} until the end of their next turn.
Additionally, you are now immune to poison and necrotic damage, and immune to the {@condition poisoned} condition.

subclassFeature.name: Spirits Domain
entries: Some priests are devoted to exploring the boundary between life and death. Spirits Domain clerics see this as their mission: tending to the bereaved, shepherding the departed, and interceding between the two when needed. They frequently worship gods of travel, death, or peace. Their vocation involves ensuring the spirits of the dead are at rest, counseling the living, and contacting the dead to help achieve those goals. Most Spirits clerics revile necromancers and others who intentionally create undead, though they are cordial or even sympathetic to the restless dead themselves.

Spirits domain priests are usually good-aligned — their basic duties require a level of care and consideration for others. Evil clerics can follow this domain, though, twisting its teachings to torment those they should protect. Very little in their background ties them together, but nearly all have experienced grief or loss, and come out with a commitment to helping those who face it.

entries.name: Spirits Domain Quirks
entries: The following are some optional quirks for a player of this domain to choose from.

rows: You speak softly and gently at all times.
You wear heavy makeup in grim, gothic designs.
You reek of ammonia and embalming fluid.
You prefer skull motifs in your vestments and jewelry.
You always carry a complete set of embalming tools.
You pray over your companions every night to keep them from dying in their sleep.

subclassFeature.name: Domain Spells
entries: You gain domain spells at the cleric levels listed in the Spirits Domain Spells table. See the {@classFeature Divine Domain|Cleric|PHB|1} class feature for how domain spells work.

entries.caption: Spirits Domain Spells
rows: 1st
{@spell spiritual consultation|KibblesTasty:CC}, {@spell unseen servant}
3rd
{@spell gentle repose}, {@spell prayer of healing}
5th
{@spell ghost step|KibblesTasty:CC}, {@spell speak with dead}
7th
{@spell death ward}, {@spell soul leech|SoManySpells}
9th
{@spell hallow}, {@spell raise dead}

subclassFeature.name: Grave Teachings
entries: At 1st level, you gain proficiency with the {@skill Religion} skill or another cleric skill of your choice if you are already proficient. You have advantage on all ability checks to recall information about death and the undead.

subclassFeature.name: Life and Death
entries: Also at 1st level, your magic is able to manipulate creatures when they are on the edge of death. You gain the following benefits:

items: When a creature below half its maximum hit points takes damage from one of your cleric spells, it takes additional damage equal to your cleric level.
When a creature below half its maximum hit points is healed by one of your cleric spells, it receives additional healing equal to your cleric level.

entries: Once a creature has been affected by this feature, it can't be affected again until after you finish a long rest.

subclassFeature.name: Channel Divinity: Back From the Brink
entries: Starting at 2nd level, you can use your {@classFeature Channel Divinity|Cleric|PHB|2} to bolster a creature's resilience. As a reaction before a creature you can see within 30 feet rolls a death saving throw, you can grant them divine fortitude. They can add your cleric level to the result. If this causes the result to be a 20 or higher, they may treat the roll as a natural 20.

subclassFeature.name: Spirit Companion
entries: Starting at 6th level, you can call upon the dead for aid, extending your abilities with the help of friendly spirits. As an action, you can summon a spirit, which appears in an unoccupied space you can see within 30 feet of you.
It is friendly to you and your companions, and obeys your commands. See its game statistics in the Spirit Companion stat block, which uses your proficiency bonus (PB) in several places.

In combat, your spirit companion shares your initiative count, taking its turn immediately after yours. It can move on its own, but the only action it takes on its turn is the {@action Dodge} action, unless you take a bonus action on your turn to command it to take another action. That action can be one in its stat block or some other action. If you are {@condition incapacitated}, it fades into the ethereal plane, reappearing in the same spot when you are no longer {@condition incapacitated}.
The spirit remains until it is reduced to 0 hit points, you use this feature to resummon it, or you die. Anything it was carrying is left behind when the spirit vanishes.

Once you summon the spirit, you can't do so again until you finish a long rest, unless you expend a use of your {@classFeature Channel Divinity|Cleric|PHB|2} to resummon it over the course of a 10-minute ritual.

subclassFeature.name: Spirits' Protection
entries: Also at 6th level, you become immune to possession or {@condition frightened||fear} effects caused by incorporeal undead.

subclassFeature.name: Spiritual Warfare
entries: Starting at 8th level, once per round, you can add your Wisdom modifier to the damage you deal with any cleric cantrip or the damage your {@subclassFeature Spirit Companion|Cleric|PHB|Spirits|SMRClerics|6} deals with its Ghostly Touch.

subclassFeature.name: Crypt Keeper
entries: At 17th level, you gain resistance to all damage inflicted by undead creatures with a challenge rating equal to or lower than your cleric level.

subclassFeature.name: Empowered Spirit
entries: Also at 17th level, your {@subclassFeature Spirit Companion|Cleric|PHB|Spirits|SMRClerics|6} grows in power. Its flying speed increases to 50 feet, and its Ghostly Touch damage increases to 2d6 + your proficiency bonus.

trait.name: Incorporeal Movement
entries: The spirit can move through other creatures and objects as if they were difficult terrain. It takes 5 ({@damage 1d10}) force damage if it ends its turn inside an object.

trait.name: Spiritual Projection
entries: The spirit can channel your holy power. Anytime it is within 30 feet of you and you can see it, you can cast spells or {@classFeature Channel Divinity|Cleric|PHB|2} as if you were in its space.

action.name: Ghostly Touch
entries: {@atk mw} {@hitYourSpellAttack} to hit, reach 5 feet, one target. {@h} {@damage 1d6 + PB} necrotic damage. The target can't regain hit points until the end of its next turn.

action.name: Divine Explosion
entries: The spirit extinguishes itself in a blaze of divine energy. All creatures within 20 feet of the spirit must make a Constitution saving throw, taking radiant damage equal to {@homebrew {@damage PBd8}|3d8} + the spirit's remaining hit points on a failure, or half as much on a success.{@homebrew | This damage increases by 1d8 whenever your proficiency bonus increases.}

items.name: Equipment
items.entry: A {@item healer's kit|PHB}, vestments, 2 glass bottles, a set of embalming tools, and a {@item pouch|PHB} containing 15 gp

entries.name: Feature: Peaceful Rest
entries: You know how to properly prepare the dead for burial. Over the course of 1 hour, you can use your embalming tools and {@item disguise kit|PHB} to protect a body from decay for 3 days. This also extends the time limit for spells such as {@spell raise dead}.

rows: Equality. Every person deserves a respectful end. (Good)
Duty. I must always improve my skill. (Neutral)
Piety. The rites are as important as the people. (Lawful)
Disdain. The dead don't actually {@i need} their grave goods. (Chaotic)
Greed. I can leverage my position to extort the grieving. (Evil)
Pride. I must ensure every afterlife is prepared perfectly. (Any)
A scroll of sacred rites is mine to protect.
One of the dead came back as a {@creature revenant}. I must atone for my failure.
I left my home to hunt a band of graverobbers.
I had a mentor I greatly respected within the cult.
My faith was challenged once, and I never found a satisfactory answer.
There's a tomb waiting for me when my time comes.
You are an insufferable know-it-all about funerary practices.
You are extremely rigid about how to treat the dead.
You have zero tolerance for desecration of graves.
You stop and inspect the embalming work on all dead or undead you find.
You have secretly lost faith in the afterlife.
A good organ should never go to waste.

entries: You were a member of a mortuary cult, a religious organization devoted to the care and preparation of the dead. You embalmed bodies, prepared tombs, and helped people pass gracefully into the afterlife. Your services were valued and essential, but not exactly sought after or beloved.
